answer:
High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and for primary prevention in adults 40-75 years with LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL or diabetes with additional risk factors [Management of Blood Cholesterol, ACC/AHA, 2018]. For primary prevention, aspirin is not routinely recommended due to bleeding risk, except in select high-risk adults 40-70 years after a risk-benefit discussion with their clinician [Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease, ACC/AHA, 2019]. 

Note: The recommendation on who should receive high-intensity statin therapy for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease may vary depending on the specific guidelines or clinical scenarios. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional and consider individual patient factors before making any treatment decisions.

Requires professional review

arguments.query: high-intensity statin therapy for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease in whom

result.query: high-intensity statin therapy for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease in whom

results.content: High-intensity statin therapy is recommended for patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and for primary prevention in adults 40-75 years with LDL-C >=190 mg/dL or diabetes with additional risk factors.

Aspirin for primary prevention of cardiovascular disease is not routinely recommended due to bleeding risk, except in select high-risk adults 40-70 years after a risk-benefit discussion with their clinician.
results.source: ACC/AHA Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease Guideline
results.citation: Primary Prevention of Cardiovascular Disease, ACC/AHA, 2019

results.content: For moderate to severe depression, a combination of antidepressant medication and high-intensity psychological therapy such as cognitive behavioral therapy is recommended over either treatment alone.
results.source: NICE Guideline on Depression in Adults
results.citation: Management of Depression in Adults, NICE, 2022

results.content: In patients with type 2 diabetes and chronic kidney disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor is recommended for those with eGFR >=20 mL/min/1.73m2 to reduce CKD progression and cardiovascular risk. ACE inhibitors or ARBs are first-line for patients with albuminuria and hypertension.
results.source: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes
results.citation: Chronic Kidney Disease in Diabetes, American Diabetes Association, 2024

results.content: For outpatient community-acquired pneumonia in healthy adults, amoxicillin or doxycycline is recommended. For inpatients, beta-lactam plus macrolide combination therapy is standard.

result.disclaimer: Evidence excerpts for professional review; not a diagnosis.
